region_of_origin: Central Asia (Iran origin); widely cultivated and sold in Singapore supermarkets
food_subgroup: Leafy green vegetable, raw. Very high vitamin K (~483 mcg/100g = 403% daily AI). Non-haem iron source with low bioavailability due to oxalate content.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of raw baby spinach is approximately 1 cup (30g), as commonly used in salads and smoothies; a larger serving of 2 cups (60g) is typical as a side salad or stir-fry base.

nutrient_source.title: Spinach, Raw — USDA SR Legacy (FDC ID 168462)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Direct match to USDA SR Legacy entry for raw spinach (Spinacia oleracea). All per-100g nutrient values sourced directly from FDC ID 168462. Baby spinach is the young-leaf form of the same species; nutrient profile is equivalent to USDA SR Legacy raw spinach reference.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson et al.; spinach GI ~15 (low)
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal) / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Spinach GI is consistently reported as approximately 15 (low) in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). Available carbohydrate per 100g = 3.6g total carbohydrate − 2.2g dietary fibre = 1.4g. GL per 100g = (15 × 1.4) ÷ 100 = 0.21, rounded to 0.2. GL per 30g serving = (15 × 0.42) ÷ 100 = 0.06, effectively negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: High potassium (558 mg/100g), magnesium (79 mg/100g), and folate (194 mcg/100g) with low naturally occurring sodium (79 mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Baby spinach is highly suitable for hypertension management: potassium (558 mg/100g) promotes renal sodium excretion and vasodilation, magnesium (79 mg/100g) supports endothelial relaxation, and folate (194 mcg/100g) reduces homocysteine-mediated vascular endothelium damage; the 79 mg sodium is naturally occurring and not from added salt.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low GI (~15) and negligible glycemic load (GL ~0.2 per 100g); magnesium supports insulin receptor signalling
type2_diabetes.guidance: Baby spinach is highly suitable for Type 2 Diabetes: GI ~15 and GL ~0.2 per 100g produce negligible postprandial glucose response, while magnesium (79 mg/100g) acts as a cofactor for insulin receptor signalling and glucose transporter function — magnesium deficiency is common in T2DM and adequate intake is associated with modest HbA1c reduction.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high vitamin K (483 mcg/100g — warfarin interaction), folate (194 mcg/100g), potassium, and negligible saturated fat (0.1 g/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Baby spinach is cardioprotective due to negligible saturated fat (0.1 g/100g), high potassium (558 mg/100g) for blood pressure support, and folate (194 mcg/100g) for homocysteine reduction; however, patients on warfarin (anticoagulation) must maintain consistent — not erratic — spinach intake due to very high vitamin K content (483 mcg/100g = 403% daily AI), as sudden large increases can antagonise anticoagulant effect.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Vitamin K (483 mcg/100g) warfarin interaction; folate (194 mcg/100g) critical for methotrexate patients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Baby spinach is suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients but requires two clinical cautions: patients on methotrexate must maintain adequate folate intake (194 mcg/100g supports this), and patients on warfarin must maintain consistent spinach intake rather than avoiding it erratically, as the very high vitamin K content (483 mcg/100g) can affect INR if intake varies significantly.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Baby Spinach (Raw) is the young-leaf form of Spinacia oleracea, a dark-green leafy vegetable with tender stems, soft delicate texture, and a mild, slightly earthy taste. It is sold pre-washed in 100g–200g bags at Singapore supermarkets including NTUC FairPrice and Cold Storage. Per 100g raw: 23 kcal, 2.9g protein, 0.4g fat, 3.6g carbohydrate, 2.2g dietary fibre, 79mg sodium. GI ~15 (very low), GL ~0.2 per 100g — negligible glycaemic impact. Nutritional highlights: Vitamin K 483 mcg/100g (403% daily AI — WARFARIN INTERACTION: patients must maintain consistent intake, not avoid erratically). Folate 194 mcg/100g (48.5% DRI — heat-labile, raw spinach preserves folate better than cooked). Iron 2.7 mg/100g — CRITICAL CAVEAT: non-haem iron with approximately 1–2% bioavailability due to high oxalate content; the 'Popeye spinach for iron' claim is nutritionally misleading as absorbed iron from spinach is negligible (~0.038 mg/100g). Adding vitamin C (e.g. lemon juice) can enhance iron absorption to approximately 5–8%. Potassium 558 mg/100g and magnesium 79 mg/100g support blood pressure and insulin signalling. Vitamin A 469 mcg RAE/100g supports immune regulation. Source: USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 168462. Confidence: high.
common_user_queries: raw spinach vs cooked spinach nutrients

confidence_reason: All per-100g nutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 168462 (Spinach, Raw), an approved primary source with full nutrient panel. Baby spinach is the young-leaf form of Spinacia oleracea; the USDA SR Legacy raw spinach entry is the standard reference for this food. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. Ontology codes verified against FoodOn, FoodEx2, and SNOMED CT. Confidence set to high.
notes: Vitamin B12 (0.0 mcg/100g) and vitamin D (0.0 mcg/100g) are confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy FDC 168462 — spinach is a plant food and naturally contains no vitamin B12 and negligible vitamin D; these are confirmed zero values, not missing data. Trans fat (0.0 g/100g) is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. Cholesterol (0.0 mg/100g) is naturally zero as spinach is a plant food. Monounsaturated fat (0.01 g/100g) and polyunsaturated fat (0.17 g/100g) are reported from USDA SR Legacy FDC 168462 fatty acid data. Water content (91.4 g/100g) sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC 168462. Iron bioavailability caveat: the 2.7 mg/100g iron figure represents total iron; actual absorbed iron is approximately 1–2% (~0.038 mg/100g) due to high oxalate content inhibiting non-haem iron absorption. Vitamin K note: USDA SR Legacy reports 482.9 mcg/100g phylloquinone (vitamin K1) — this is among the highest of any common food and represents a clinically significant warfarin interaction risk requiring consistent rather than erratic intake. Folate is heat-labile; raw spinach preserves folate better than boiled or steamed preparations. All three portions listed in input (30g, 60g, 100g) are valid reference portions for this food.
